claim: The western cliff coast (Odeceixe to Sagres) is wild, rugged, and never overcrowded — combined with the Vicentine Coast Natural Park, this represents a zone of high physical hazard (cliffs) and minimal human presence for lost dog searches.
stance_target: western cliff coast as combined cliff-hazard and low-sighting-probability zone
scope_conditions: Costa Vicentina, Parque Natural da Sudoeste Alentejano e Costa Vincentina
quoted_support: From Odeceixe in the north, down to historical Cape St. Vincent and to Sagres, the landscape is rugged, wild and imposing; never overcrowded, this grand scenery invites you to walk the cliff-tops... the lengthy Vicentine Coast Natural Park provides shelter to a wealth of soaring and marine birds, endangered botanic species and rare amphibians.
entities: Parque Natural da Sudoeste Alentejano e Costa Vincentina

claim: Alvor estuary contains rich marshland — a distinct wetland habitat near a tourist town (Portimão-Lagos corridor) where a lost dog could find food and water in a semi-urban/natural interface.
stance_target: Alvor as estuarine food/water attractor near human settlement
quoted_support: In Alvor you'll also find an inviting estuary and a rich marshland.

claim: Serra de Monchique has abundant small freshwater streams particularly around Caldas de Monchique — permanent year-round water sources in the western mountain zone that would attract lost dogs.
stance_target: Caldas de Monchique streams as permanent water attractors in mountain zone
scope_conditions: Serra de Monchique, Caldas de Monchique area
quoted_support: The Monchique mountains are the highest in Algarve... The mountain range is characterized by its dense vegetation cover and by the presence of abundant, little freshwater streams, particularly around the village of Caldas de Monchique.

claim: The eastern Algarve (Guadiana valley) is much drier than western Algarve despite the river — schist rock, holm oak, stone pine, steep crags — and is described as more remote, with a very different landscape from Monchique.
stance_target: eastern Serra Caldeirão as resource-scarcer, more remote zone than western Monchique
quoted_support: along the upland, oriental extreme of Algarve, the landscape is much, much drier, despite the presence of a large river valley. The winding River Guadiana, flowing throughout schist rock faces, has created an unforgiving but appealing landscape, made of curled hilltops and steep crags, sparsely covered with holm oak, stone pine, olive groves and traditional groves of almond and carob trees. A somewhat more remote place within the Algarve.

claim: Cork oak forests in the Serra contain high biodiversity including mammals, reptiles, amphibians, and butterflies — suggesting that cork oak zones act as both corridors and food-rich refuges for wildlife including lost dogs.
stance_target: cork oak zones as high-biodiversity wildlife movement corridors
quoted_support: Cork oak forests compose a quite scarce and specific ecosystem with stunning biodiversity, including not only birds, but also a large number of amphibians, reptiles, mammals and butterflies.

claim: Ria Formosa Natural Park has Mediterranean Chameleon habitat at Quinta do Lago/Ludo (umbrella pines as refuge) — a highly specific microhabitat within a tourist zone, indicating that protected natural areas can exist within heavily developed coastal regions.
stance_target: Ria Formosa as ecologically complex zone mixing tourism and wildlife
scope_conditions: Quinta do Lago, Quinta do Ludo, Faro
quoted_support: shading the watery surface near Quinta do Lago and Quinta do Ludo, in Faro, elegant umbrella pines serve as a last refuge for the endangered Mediterranean Chameleon.
